items.why: Students reverse IV and DV. The IV is what you CHANGE (input); the DV is what you MEASURE (outcome). Mnemonic: 'I change the I.'

items.q: What does a correlation of -0.8 indicate?
items.misconception: A weak relationship (because it is negative).
items.why: Students treat the negative sign as 'weak'. The sign shows DIRECTION (as one variable goes up, the other goes down); the absolute value shows STRENGTH. -0.8 is strong.

items.q: When is deception in research ever acceptable?
items.correct: When demand characteristics would otherwise invalidate the study, with thorough debrief and ethics approval.
items.why: Students think BPS guidelines forbid deception outright. They actually permit it when justified, minimised, and followed by full debrief allowing withdrawal of data.

items.q: Picking the first 20 students who walk past you is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Opportunity. “Random” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which ethical principle means knowing what the study involves before agreeing?
items.why: Correct answer: Informed consent. “Confidentiality” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many key sampling methods are commonly listed at GCSE?
items.why: Exact answer: 4. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
